metadata.seed: I just feel Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.

baseline.patient_query: I just feel Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.
baseline.baseline_response: I’m really sorry you’re feeling stuck—feeling like you’re just “going to be” a passive recipient of support can be exhausting. It might help to start with small, low‑commitment experiments: volunteer, take an online course in something that sparks curiosity, or set a tiny daily goal that feels like a step toward a new direction. Even a few hours a week of exploration can gradually reveal a path that feels meaningful, and you’ll have a clearer picture of whether business or something else is right for you.

baseline.patient_query: I guess I do feel a bit lost and overwhelmed. It's just that every time I think about applying for jobs or even updating my resume, I get this knot in my stomach. I want to feel in control, but it just seems so daunting... Maybe just looking at some job listings? That feels manageable, I suppose. But then I worry about not measuring up or making the right choice.
baseline.baseline_response: It’s completely normal to feel that knot when the job hunt feels like a giant mountain—many people get stuck in the same place. Try breaking the process into tiny, doable chunks: spend just 10 minutes a day scrolling through listings, then set aside a separate slot to tweak your résumé for one of those roles. Remember, each small step is progress, and you’re not alone in feeling overwhelmed.

baseline.patient_query: I think just browsing them, like seeing what’s out there, feels okay. It’s kind of a distraction, too, from all the pressure I feel. But then I get stuck on what to even look for—I mean, do I go for something similar to what I had before, or try something new? And then I worry about whether I’ll even be good enough for whatever I choose.
baseline.baseline_response: It’s totally normal to feel stuck when you’re trying to decide what to look at next—just remember that browsing is a low‑stakes way to explore without pressure. Start by jotting down a few themes or styles that catch your eye, then sample a handful of pieces that fit those ideas, and see how you feel. And don’t let the “good enough” worry hold you back; every new option you try is a chance to learn and grow, not a test of your worth.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it’s definitely hard to shake that feeling. I guess I’ve always been told that if you want to succeed, you have to really stand out, you know? I think I just carry that pressure with me. It makes me feel like I have to prove something, like if I don’t excel right away, I’ll just be a failure. It’s exhausting, honestly.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy weight of expectation, and that’s exhausting. Remember that success isn’t just about instant excellence—it’s about learning, growing, and being kind to yourself along the way. Try setting small, achievable goals and celebrate each step; that can help shift the focus from proving yourself to nurturing your own progress.

baseline.patient_query: I don’t know, maybe it’s just what I’ve seen in my life—like, people around me always seemed to jump into things and do well right off the bat. I guess I feel like I should just know what I’m doing and have it all figured out. When that doesn’t happen, it's like I'm letting everyone down, including myself. It just makes every step feel like a bigger deal than it probably is.
baseline.baseline_response: It’s completely understandable to feel that pressure when everyone else seems to hit the ground running; the reality is that most people are still learning, just like you. Perfection isn’t a prerequisite for progress—each small step you take is a valuable part of the journey. Remember that letting yourself be human and making mistakes is what ultimately builds confidence and resilience.

baseline.patient_query: I suppose it’s not that anyone's actually said anything to me directly, but... I just kind of feel that way sometimes. Like I’m not living up to expectations—mine and those of people around me. It’s more of a nagging thought, you know? It’s like this fear that if I don’t succeed right away, they’ll be disappointed, even if they haven’t expressed that. It’s hard to shake that feeling.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy, invisible weight that’s hard to shake, and that can feel exhausting. Remember that expectations—both yours and others’—are often more fluid than they appear, and success isn’t always measured in instant results. Giving yourself permission to progress at your own pace and checking in with how you truly feel can help ease that nagging fear.

baseline.patient_query: I guess I don’t have concrete evidence, more like a sense of it. I’ve seen how people celebrate achievements, and it seems like there’s always this rush to “do well” in life. It’s just... I kind of internalize that, thinking if I don’t fit that mold, I’ll somehow let them down. It's silly, right? But it feels so real in the moment. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just imagining things, but it still sticks with me.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy, invisible weight that makes you feel like you’re constantly being judged by an unseen standard. That pressure can feel both absurd and all‑too real, especially when it starts to shape how you see yourself and your worth. Remember that it’s okay to define success on your own terms—your value isn’t tied to anyone else’s applause.

baseline.patient_query: I guess it’s more about feelings than actual proof. Like, when my friends or family talk about their successes, I can’t help but compare myself. And then if I talk about my struggles, even if they’re supportive, I still feel this weight as if my journey isn’t... good enough. It’s like I’m scared that if I admit to feeling lost, they’ll think less of me, even if they don’t say it. It’s frustrating because I know it’s probably not
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy self‑critique that makes even supportive words feel like a reminder of what you think you’re missing. Remember that everyone compares themselves at some point, and the fear of being judged often outweighs the reality of others’ empathy. Try to pause and reframe those moments—acknowledge the comparison without letting it define you, and gently remind yourself that your journey is valid, even if it doesn’t match anyone else’s timeline.

baseline.patient_query: It’s kind of a mix of fear and vulnerability, I guess. I feel this tightness in my chest when I think about opening up—like, what if they don’t understand? Or worse, what if they start to see me differently? It makes me hesitate, even though I know they care. It just feels safer to keep it all inside, even if it gets heavy.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight, and that tightness in your chest is a natural reaction to the fear of being misunderstood or changed. It’s okay to feel hesitant—opening up is a big step, and you deserve to feel safe in the process. Consider sharing just a small piece of what’s on your mind first; sometimes a tiny glimpse can ease the pressure and show that their care is real.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, that tightness is definitely there. I think what really gets to me is the thought that if I share my struggles, people will see me as weak or not capable. Like, I don’t want to be seen as someone who can’t handle things. It feels like a risk—what if they don’t see me the same way afterward? That fear can really keep me from being honest about how overwhelmed I feel.
baseline.baseline_response: It’s completely understandable to feel that way—sharing vulnerability can feel risky, especially when you’re worried about being judged. Remember, opening up about what’s overwhelming you isn’t a sign of weakness; it’s often a courageous act that can invite empathy and support. If you’re hesitant, try starting with someone you trust or in a small, safe setting, and you’ll likely find that honesty can actually strengthen the connection rather than diminish it.
